resources.string: Tinnitus masker sound generator with 16 adjustable frequency bands
Select duration of the saved file

string-array.name: help_steps
string-array.item: Use the 16 sliders at the bottom of the screen to adjust the intensity of each noise frequency band from 0 to 100. Next to each slider is a number box where you can type the exact value directly; the slider and the number box stay in sync automatically.
Use the “Master Volume” slider to raise or lower the overall loudness of the generated sound.
Use the “Left Ear” and “Right Ear” sliders to adjust the sound level independently for each headphone channel.
Tap “Play” to start playback with the current settings; tap the same button again (now labeled “Stop”) to stop it.
Tap “Save Sound” to render the current settings into a WAV audio file of a chosen duration and save it on your device.
Use “Playback Schedule” to set start/end times and days of the week for automatic playback (e.g. every night while you sleep).
All your settings (16 bands, master volume, left/right volume) are saved automatically; closing and reopening the app restores your last settings.

The “Tonal Masker” tab lets you use pure sine tones at 16 frequencies instead of noise; this mode is better suited for matching your exact tinnitus pitch, and its settings, playback, and saving are completely independent from the “Noise Masker” tab.
The “Audiogram” tab lets you run a headphone-based hearing test that charts both ears\' hearing; the chart can be saved and shared, but it is not a substitute for a professional hearing assessment.

In the “Noise Masker” tab, the “Remove Tinnitus Frequency” section lets you enter your tinnitus pitch (or pull it from your audiogram result) so that exact band is removed from the noise; this is based on notched sound research (Pantev et al.).
During the audiogram test, use “Pause Test” to pause at any time, and “Resume Test” to continue right where you left off.

resources.string: In tonal masker mode, instead of filtered noise, pure sine tones are generated at 16 frequencies. You can select one or more frequencies close to your tinnitus pitch and adjust their intensity.

This test lets you produce a preliminary hearing chart (audiogram) for both ears using headphones. It is based on the Hughson-Westlake method, the standard clinical procedure for finding hearing thresholds.

Before you start:
1. Make sure to use headphones (not the phone speaker).
2. Find a quiet environment.
3. Use the button below to play a test tone and set your volume to a comfortable, clearly audible level (not too loud).
4. Then start the test; for each ear, tones will play at 6 standard frequencies (250-8000 Hz). Tap “I heard it” as soon as you hear a tone. If you don't hear anything, do nothing and it will move on automatically.

Note: this is a personal, uncalibrated screening test (dependent on your headphone model, volume setting, and ambient noise) and is not a substitute for clinical hearing assessment by an audiologist. Use the results only to compare and track your own trend over time.
Play Test Tone to Set Volume
Tap the button below as soon as you hear the sound. If you don\'t hear anything, do nothing.
Red circle = right ear, blue X = left ear. A higher point means that frequency was heard at a quieter level (relatively better hearing).

Based on notched sound research (Pantev et al., 2012): removing a narrow band exactly at your tinnitus pitch from the noise may reduce tinnitus perception. Note: long-term scientific evidence for this method is still not conclusive; treat it as an experimental option to try.
You need to complete a hearing test first (from the Audiogram tab)
Please enter a valid frequency between 60 and 16000 Hz
Frequency %1$s Hz retrieved from audiogram result
